title: Question about the weather
wikitext:
[]: Question about the weather.             Quote Post by BornIn1142 » Sun Jun 12, 2011 3:38 am How much do we know about the post-2I climate changes around the world? If the planet's axis has tilted so - I'm guessing - Japan has moved closer to the equator, wouldn't that indicate that the other side of the planet has shifted away from it and is accordingly more temperate? Or is it eternal summer everywhere? Is Arctica doing all right?

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Sun Jun 12, 2011 4:05 am We know very little. Japan's weather is the only one consistently described. However, America is apparently starving, so it's likely it's not doing very well for itself. On the other hand, it might be a lack of a work force and not a result of weather. A thought experiment I am aware of (Jaredia) rotated the axis of the Earth such that the Americas, Antarctica, western Australia and eastern Asia were tropical. If you are willing to risk a few NSFW pictures, you can see more about the relative climate of that hypothetical world. Of course, that is only one possible configuration and the Earth can be shifted in dozens of ways while keeping Japan tropical, so I wouldn't use it as the "Definitive Guide to Post-2I Climates", ESPECIALLY because it includes Antarctica in its considerations of currents and wind directions.

[]: Re: Question about the weather.             Quote Post by SaltyJoe » Sun Jun 12, 2011 4:07 am BornIn1142 wrote: How much do we know about the post-2I climate changes around the world?

[]:             Quote Post by Trajan » Sun Jun 12, 2011 1:26 pm Are there any good screenshots of the planet in NGE that might help determine the new location of the landmasses?

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Sun Jun 12, 2011 4:07 pm Nope. I can only think of two right now anyway: the lights going out all over Japan (which looks suspiciously unsunk) and the spear coming out towards Arael (blocked by cloud cover).

[]: Re: Question about the weather.             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sun Jun 12, 2011 8:59 pm BornIn1142 wrote: How much do we know about the post-2I climate changes around the world? If the planet's axis has tilted so - I'm guessing - Japan has moved closer to the equator, wouldn't that indicate that the other side of the planet has shifted away from it and is accordingly more temperate? Or is it eternal summer everywhere? Is Arctica doing all right?

[]: Re: Question about the weather.             Quote Post by SaltyJoe » Mon Jun 13, 2011 3:50 am Bagheera wrote: -- Russia (specifically Siberia) is the breadbasket of the world.

[]: Re: Question about the weather.             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Jun 13, 2011 4:11 am SaltyJoe wrote: I'm no expert on the subject, but wouldn't fiteen years be a bit too fast to make the transition from mostly useless taiga soil into something highly fertile?

[]: Re: Question about the weather.             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Mon Jun 13, 2011 4:56 am Bagheera wrote: It really depends. Once the climate shifts and people start moving up there it could become suitable in a hurry. And don't forget, this is a setting where everything from soul harvesting tech to giant robots to telescoping fortress cities are possible. Soil enrichment seems a pittance by comparison.

[]: Re: Question about the weather.             Quote Post by SaltyJoe » Mon Jun 13, 2011 11:13 am Bagheera wrote: (also, Siberia's a lot more ecologically diverse than people seem to think it is; you have everything from boreal forest to grassland to peat bogs to temperate forest to choose from, with the barren taiga only really an issue in the far north. Think Minnesota/Wisconsin and southern Canada and you'll see what I'm getting at.)

[]: Re: Question about the weather.             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Jun 13, 2011 1:58 pm SaltyJoe wrote: But this topic got me thinking about NGE's undiclosed history more than i think i should bother: for example, what about the Sahara? The water that got into circulation after the Antarctic icecaps melted (or blew up, more precisely) might result in greater precipitation in some areas of the world. And a greening Sahara is possibly an even better bet for breaking new lands for crop then Siberia.

[]: Re: Question about the weather.             Quote Post by Fireand'chutes77 » Mon Jun 13, 2011 9:33 pm SaltyJoe wrote: But this topic got me thinking about NGE's undiclosed history more than i think i should bother..... Eh, it's not like the show itself cares about such details.

[]: Re: Question about the weather.             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Jun 13, 2011 9:46 pm Fireand'chutes77 wrote: It does make excellent fodder for an spinoff, though. I always wondered how people were coping in, say, Western Europe or on the American Eastern Seaboard. (I mention the Eastern Seaboard because I live in Virginia, heh. Given the extent the Chesapeake Bay and James River carve through the state, we'd have problems after those 2I tsunamis...)

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Mon Jun 13, 2011 11:51 pm Actually, the teacher says that sea levels rose. The easiest explanation for this is that Adam's explosion produced a lot of matter to contribute to the Dead Sea.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Jun 14, 2011 5:45 am Legendary wrote: Actually, the teacher says that sea levels rose. The easiest explanation for this is that Adam's explosion produced a lot of matter to contribute to the Dead Sea.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue Jun 14, 2011 7:09 am Sea levels rose substantially, but most of Japan underwent rapid geological uplift to compensate.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Jun 14, 2011 4:53 pm Reichu wrote: Sea levels rose substantially, but most of Japan underwent rapid geological uplift to compensate.

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Tue Jun 14, 2011 4:56 pm First off, we see several sunken cities, so parts of Japan are underwater. Second off, Japan already IS pretty high up. The coastal cities will be engulfed but surprisingly that is pretty much it. The terrain is too mountainous to properly sink.
